preFix.title: Spinning Herdwick fleece
preFix.subtitle: Preparing and spinning the coarse, hairy fleece of the Herdwick breed into a hardwearing two-ply yarn
preFix.excerpt: Herdwick is one of the coarsest British mountain breeds, with a hairy, long-stapled fleece that spins into a hardwearing yarn suited to outerwear and floor rugs. This entry covers selecting, preparing, and spinning Herdwick into a balanced two-ply.
preFix.sourceNotes: Herdwick fleece preparation and spinning synthesised from general coarse-breed spinning practice. The Herdwick is a Lake District mountain breed; its fleece characteristics are well documented in British spinning literature.

content.text: Herdwick is the iconic fell sheep of the Lake District. Its fleece is at the coarser end of the British mountain breed range: the outer coat is long, hairy, and weather-resistant, while the inner coat is softer and shorter. The fleece typically measures 30-35 microns and has a
staple length
of 8-15 cm. Most Herdwick fleece contains some
kemp
: the short, opaque white fibres that give the finished yarn its characteristic salt-and-pepper halo. Kemp cannot be carded out entirely; it is a feature of the breed's character, not a flaw.

attrs.body: Carding Herdwick throws fibre dust. Work in a ventilated room and damp-mop the floor afterwards. People with asthma should wear a dust mask.
attrs.title: Wool dust caution

items.name: Raw Herdwick fleece, skirted and washed

content.text: Method
Step 1: Select and wash the fleece

Herdwick fleece is robust and can withstand a thorough hot wash without matting as badly as finer breeds. Soak the washed and skirted fleece in hot water (60°C) with a small amount of washing-up liquid for 20 minutes without agitating. Drain and rinse twice at the same temperature. The fleece will look dark grey to charcoal when wet; it lightens to a mid grey when dry. Spread flat on a mesh drying rack.
Step 2: Card into rolags

Use hand cards with a coarse tooth setting (54-72 teeth per inch) suitable for the long-stapled coarse fibre. Load the carder in the direction of the staple (tip to cut end). Card in short strokes, transferring fibre between the two cards 3-4 times per preparation. Roll off the carded fibre as a loose
woollen-draw
rolag. Herdwick cards more easily than sheep with shorter, denser fleeces; the long staple means the fibres align relatively readily.
Step 3: Spin the singles
Set the wheel to a moderate tension and a relatively high twist ratio for the coarse fibre: Herdwick needs more twist to hold the long fibres together than fine merino would. Spin using the
woollen draw

: draft the fibre away from the orifice with one hand while the other controls the flow from the rolag. Allow the twist to run into the drafting zone before drawing back. The singles should be firm and slightly over-twisted to compensate for the twist loss during plying. Aim for an aran-weight single.
Step 4: Ply and set the twist
Wind two full bobbins of singles and ply them together in the S-direction (opposite to the Z-twist of the singles) at the same wheel setting. The
two-ply

should be balanced: a cut length held mid-air should hang without excess kinking in either direction. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy and tie the skein loosely. Soak in warm water for 20 minutes, squeeze gently, snap sharply between both hands twice to set the ply, and hang to dry under light tension. The finished yarn will be a firm, hardwearing aran-weight with a natural grey colour and a slight halo from the kemp fibres.

items.fix: Increase the tension slightly and slow down the drafting hand to allow more twist into the yarn before drawing back. Herdwick needs more twist per inch than fine wools.
items.cause: The twist ratio is too low for the coarse, long fibre
items.problem: The singles are weak and break during spinning

items.fix: Accept the halo: it is a characteristic of the breed. If a smoother yarn is needed, use the combed top preparation from a breed supplier rather than carding the raw fleece.
items.cause: This is normal for Herdwick and cannot be prevented
items.problem: The kemp fibres are poking out of the finished yarn in coarse spikes

items.fix: Hold a cut length under tension: a balanced ply shows no excess twist. Reduce the ply tension or increase plying speed to reduce ply twist.
items.cause: Too much twist was added during plying relative to the singles twist
items.problem: The ply is over-twisted and kinky

content.text: Use and characteristics

Herdwick yarn is not suited to garments worn directly against skin but is excellent for outerwear, bags, hats, and floor rugs. The natural grey colour can be left undyed or overdyed with plant dyes; iron and copper mordants shift the grey toward olive or brown. The fleece is one of the most sustainable British wools in terms of production conditions: Herdwick sheep live year-round on open fells with minimal supplementary feeding.
